{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks about the completion of calcification of the roots of deciduous teeth. From the provided documents, the most relevant information is in Document [6] (Title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Embryology), which discusses the eruption and development timeline of primary teeth. It states that the primary teeth erupt when crown mineralization is mostly complete but before full root formation. The primary teeth are shed between 6 to 7 years of age, and their permanent successors erupt around 6 to 8 years of age. This implies that root formation in deciduous teeth is completed before the tooth is shed. Based on dental embryology, root calcification of primary teeth is generally completed by around 6 years of age. This is supported by the fact that by age 6–7, primary teeth are exfoliated, indicating that root development was complete by that time. Therefore, the completion of root calcification occurs by 6 years.", "answer_choice": "C"}
